us-politics.headline: Trump Threatens Iran With Strikes, Rescues Downed Airman
us-politics.body:
President Trump issued a profane Easter Sunday warning to Iran, threatening to bomb power plants and bridges if Tehran does not reopen the Strait of Hormuz. The threat came as US forces, assisted by a CIA deception campaign, rescued an American airman shot down over Iranian territory - a rare successful recovery behind enemy lines.

A retired Army general predicted the conflict could conclude in under 60 days, while a former World Bank chief warned Iran's economy is collapsing toward a barter system. Jet fuel costs have spiked sharply, hitting airlines and travelers hard.

Democrats and critics questioned the legal basis for military escalation. Supporters pointed to the rescue as proof of US operational capability. The Strait of Hormuz, one congressman noted, may matter more strategically than Iran's nuclear program.

sources.title: House Democrat: Iran’s control of Strait of Hormuz ‘more strategically vital’ than nuclear weapon
Trump threatens Iran with ‘Hell’ over Strait of Hormuz in profane post
Trump drops Easter Sunday f-bomb in new threat to Iran
Trump vows US will strike Iran’s power plants, bridges if Strait of Hormuz is not reopened
US rescues missing service member from downed jet in Iran: Trump
Retired Army general predicts Iran war wrapped up in ‘less than 60 days’
Former World Bank chief says Iran economy collapsing into ‘barter system’ amid conflict
Jet fuel costs skyrocket amid Iran war, exacerbating crisis for airlines, travelers
Trump says U.S. military has rescued airman shot down over Iran

us-politics.headline: Trump Fires Bondi, Raising Questions About DOJ Independence
us-politics.body:
President Trump dismissed Attorney General Pam Bondi, a move that drew immediate criticism from Senate Democrats. Senator Tim Kaine called the firing a warning to whoever takes the job next, arguing it signals the attorney general serves at the president's political pleasure rather than as an independent law enforcement officer.

NPR reported the dismissal as part of a broader pattern of Trump breaking campaign commitments. Bondi had been a close ally. Her removal nonetheless surprised many observers who expected her tenure to be stable.

The firing adds to ongoing debate about executive control over federal institutions. Critics on the left see a pattern of politicising the Justice Department. Supporters of the president argue he has the constitutional authority to shape his own cabinet.

sources.title: Kaine: Bondi firing ‘should be a lesson’ for next attorney general nominee
Politics chat: Trump fires Attorney General Bondi, seeks to break campaign promises

us-politics.headline: Courts, Elections, and Emergency Powers Draw Fresh Scrutiny
us-politics.body:
A federal judge blocked the Trump administration's effort to collect race-based admissions data from universities, the latest in a series of court checks on White House directives. Separately, a new ad campaign - backed by Republicans - pushed back against Trump's stated interest in federalising election administration.

Opinion writers at The Hill argued Trump's use of emergency powers has itself become the emergency, citing the administration's push to restore ballroom construction at a federal site as one example of emergency declarations being stretched beyond their intended scope.

The pattern across these disputes is consistent: the administration moves aggressively on executive authority, courts and some Republicans resist, and the legal battles accumulate. Where those battles ultimately land remains unsettled.

sources.title: Trump admin urges restoring ballroom construction in emergency motion: 'Time is of the essence'
Trump’s abuse of emergency powers is the real national emergency
Federal judge blocks Trump push to collect race-based admissions data
New ad campaign speaks out against Trump’s threat to ‘federalize’ elections

us-politics.headline: Pope Leo Calls for Peace; NATO Fractures; Harris Eyes 2028
us-politics.body:
Pope Leo used his first Easter address to deliver a direct call for peace, with the Washington Post noting the message landed pointedly against a backdrop of active US military engagement in Iran. In Europe, Spain's decision to ban US military aircraft from its airspace prompted analysts to question whether NATO's universal alliance model is effectively finished.

In Hungary, both Trump and Putin are reportedly backing Viktor Orban ahead of elections, a pairing that has drawn attention to the administration's alignment with nationalist governments abroad.

On the domestic political horizon, Fox News observed that Kamala Harris has been travelling and speaking in ways that strongly suggest a 2028 presidential run is under consideration.

sources.title: On Easter, Pope Leo delivers commanding message of peace to a world at war
The ‘post-global’ NATO: Spain’s airspace ban signals the death of universal alliances
In Hungarian election, Trump and Putin are backing Viktor Orban
Kamala Harris' travels and comments clearly point to 2028

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Bites Farmers as Japan Pledges Normal Supply
au-politics.body:
Australia's fuel crisis is squeezing farmers and households hard, with some growers saying they cannot afford to put food on the table. Used electric vehicle prices are rising in response, with buyers increasingly unwilling to stay locked into combustion engines as costs climb.

Japan's Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi has moved to reassure Canberra that Australia will receive normal fuel supplies, offering a degree of diplomatic relief amid the disruption. The assurance follows growing anxiety about energy security across the country.

The crisis is reshaping consumer behaviour fast. Secondhand EV demand is surging even as supply remains tight, suggesting the fuel shock may be accelerating a shift that policy alone had struggled to drive.

sources.title: ‘Doesn’t make sense to hold onto a combustion engine’: used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits
'Struggling massively': Farmer finds it hard to put food on the table
Japan assures Australia will get 'normal supply' of fuel

au-politics.headline: Aboriginal Child Removal Ruling Highlights Ongoing Custody Tensions
au-politics.body:
A family court has ordered the return of an Aboriginal child moved 1,700 kilometres from their remote Northern Territory community, a ruling that cuts to the heart of longstanding disputes over Indigenous child welfare and state intervention.

The case draws fresh attention to the tension between child protection frameworks and the rights of Aboriginal families and communities to keep children connected to country and culture. Critics have long argued that removal practices echo the patterns of earlier generations.

The court's decision is a clear rebuke to whoever authorised the move. It will likely intensify scrutiny of how child protection agencies handle cases involving remote Indigenous communities.

sources.title: Aboriginal child moved 1,700km from remote NT community should be returned, family court rules

au-politics.headline: Tasmanian Forest Logged After Swift Parrot Calls Recorded
au-politics.body:
A patch of Tasmanian forest was logged after researchers recorded dozens of calls from the critically endangered swift parrot in the area. The bird, already under severe pressure from habitat loss, relies on old-growth forest for nesting and feeding.

The logging has drawn sharp criticism from conservationists who argue the recordings should have triggered immediate protection. Defenders of the industry point to existing approvals and legal processes. The clash reflects a broader and unresolved conflict between forestry operations and wildlife protection in Tasmania.

The swift parrot is considered one of Australia's most at-risk bird species. Its population has declined steeply in recent decades.

sources.title: The swift parrot’s distinctive call was recorded dozens of times in a patch of Tasmanian forest. Then the forest was logged

au-politics.headline: Police Negligence Lawsuit Looms After Officer Killed by Freeman
au-politics.body:
The sisters of a police officer killed by Dezi Freeman are preparing to sue the force for negligence, according to their lawyer. The case centres on whether police failed in their duty of care in circumstances that led to the officer's death.

The lawsuit, if it proceeds, will put internal police decision-making under legal scrutiny. Negligence claims against police forces are rare but not unprecedented in Australia, and outcomes can carry significant implications for operational procedures and accountability standards.

Freeman's case has already attracted public attention. A civil action by the officer's family would extend that scrutiny well beyond the criminal proceedings.

sources.title: Sisters of officer killed by Dezi Freeman plan to sue police for negligence, lawyer says

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Escalates as Hormuz Crisis Deepens
world.body:
Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz and continues striking Gulf infrastructure, hitting Kuwait's power and water facilities as the conflict shows no sign of ending. Trump threatened further punishment for Iran but offered no concrete plan, while Europe scrambles over disrupted energy supplies. Italian PM Meloni called the Gulf fundamental to European security.

Diplomats are searching for off-ramps. Former Iranian FM Zarif proposed a peace roadmap, and Oman confirmed talks with Tehran over restoring Hormuz transit. Political scientist John Mearsheimer sees no quick resolution. Meanwhile, Nepal announced two-day weekends to conserve fuel, and thousands rallied in Baghdad against what protesters called a senseless war. Iran's sports minister warned the country may miss the 2026 World Cup if matches aren't relocated from the US.

sources.title: Trump threatens ‘hell’ for Iran over Hormuz Strait as deadline approaches
Mearsheimer: No signs of quick end to US-Israel war on Iran
Why an attack on Bushehr nuclear plant would be catastrophic for the Gulf
Nepal announces two-day weekends as fuel crisis caused by Iran war deepens
Trump offers no concrete plan as Strait of Hormuz closure shocks Europe
Iran’s ex-FM Zarif proposes peace roadmap; Gulf points at erosion of trust
Italian PM says the Gulf is ‘fundamental’ for Europe’s security
Oman, Iran discuss smooth transit in Strait of Hormuz, Muscat says
Kuwait’s power, water plants damaged as Iran keeps attacking Gulf states
Thousands rally in Iraq against ‘senseless’ US-Israel war on Iran
Trump seeks to end the war without it becoming ‘an abject failure’
Iran’s World Cup participation depends on relocation from US, minister says

world.headline: US Airman Rescued from Inside Iran After F-15 Downed
world.body:
A US airman was rescued from inside Iranian territory after his F-15E was shot down during the ongoing conflict, Trump confirmed. The pilot was injured but safe. Details of the rescue operation - involving coordination under hostile conditions deep inside Iran - are emerging, with the BBC reporting on how the mission was executed.

The incident marks a rare and dangerous episode of direct US personnel exposure on Iranian soil, raising the stakes in an already volatile conflict.

sources.title: How downed F-15 US airman was rescued inside Iran
US pilot from downed F-15E plane rescued in Iran: What we know
US airman injured but safe after rescue from inside Iran, Trump says

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Captures Moon and Earth on Historic Mission
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew, including Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen - the first Canadian to travel to the Moon - is sending back striking imagery of Earth receding and the lunar surface up close. The mission has produced photographs that rival the iconic shots from Apollo.

Debate has emerged over whether the images serve science or function more as spectacular public relations. Either way, the mission marks a significant milestone in NASA's return to crewed lunar exploration.

sources.title: Earth in rear-view of Artemis II astronauts
Artemis's stunning Moon pictures - science or holiday photos?
Jeremy Hansen is the first Canadian to head to the moon

world.headline: Measles Kills 100 Children in Bangladesh; Lebanon Marks Grim Easter
world.body:
A suspected measles outbreak has killed nearly 100 children in Bangladesh, raising alarm over vaccination gaps in densely populated areas. Health authorities are working to contain the spread.

In Lebanon, Christian families displaced by ongoing conflict marked Easter away from their homes. Separately, Pope Leo used his first Easter Mass to call on world leaders to choose peace - a message carrying particular weight as multiple conflicts grind on across the Middle East and Eastern Europe, where Ukraine struck a Russian oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod and a port in Primorsk.

sources.title: Christian Lebanese families displaced as they celebrate Easter
Suspected measles outbreak kills nearly 100 children in Bangladesh
Pope Leo calls for global leaders to choose peace in his first Easter Mass
Ukraine hits port in Russia’s Primorsk, oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod

ai.headline: AI Tools Hedge Their Bets While Users Pay the Price
ai.body:
Microsoft's terms of service now explicitly classify Copilot as entertainment, warning users not to rely on it for anything important - a striking disclaimer from a company aggressively selling AI productivity tools to businesses and consumers alike. The fine print exposes a widening gap between marketing claims and legal reality.

Meanwhile, Suno's AI music generator is drawing fresh scrutiny over copyright, with The Verge reporting the platform creates a legal minefield around ownership, licensing, and artist rights. Grammarly is facing its own credibility crisis after its AI review features produced embarrassing errors. Across the board, AI products are shipping fast while accountability lags badly behind.

sources.title: Microsoft terms say Copilot is for entertainment purposes only, not serious use
Suno is a music copyright nightmare
Grammarly’s sloppelganger saga

ai.headline: Robots Fill Japan's Labor Gaps as AI Finds Real-World Footing
ai.body:
Japan is deploying physical AI robots in roles that workers simply won't take - warehouse logistics, elder care, and hazardous tasks - turning a demographic crisis into a proving ground for embodied AI. TechCrunch reports the results are surprisingly practical, with robots handling real-world variability better than expected.

Separately, Google's Gemini integration in Maps is winning over skeptics. A hands-on test found the AI-planned itinerary genuinely useful, suggesting that narrow, well-scoped AI applications outperform the broad assistant pitch. Both cases point to the same lesson: AI works best when it solves a specific, concrete problem rather than trying to replace human judgment wholesale.

sources.title: In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants
I let Gemini in Google Maps plan my day and it went surprisingly well

ai.headline: Artemis II Circles the Moon With Few Complaints
ai.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew captured the first human-observed views of the Moon's far side, marking a milestone not seen since Apollo. The mission is progressing smoothly enough that coverage has turned to minor oddities - including frozen urine venting from the spacecraft, which Ars Technica covered with appropriate deadpan.

The lack of drama is itself the story. A crewed lunar flyby proceeding on schedule, with the biggest headlines being bathroom logistics, signals a meaningful shift in how routine deep-space human spaceflight is becoming.

sources.title: Artemis II crew see first glimpse of far side of Moon [video]
Artemis II is going so well that we're left to talk about frozen urine

ai.headline: Security Stumbles: CBP Codes Leak, BrowserStack Exposes Emails
ai.body:
Sensitive U.S. Customs and Border Protection facility codes appear to have leaked through online flashcard study sets, according to Ars Technica - an embarrassing operational security failure with real implications for border infrastructure. The leak likely came from employees using consumer apps to memorize internal codes.

In a separate incident, a developer discovered BrowserStack is leaking user email addresses, pointing to a server-side misconfiguration or insider access problem. Neither breach involves exotic hacking. Both are the kind of mundane, preventable exposure that security teams keep failing to stop.

sources.title: CBP facility codes sure seem to have leaked via online flashcards
Someone at BrowserStack Is Leaking Users' Email Address

btc.headline: North Korea Behind $270M Drift Protocol Heist
btc.body:
Drift Protocol has confirmed that a $270-280 million exploit was the work of North Korean state-linked hackers, following six months of deliberate preparation and infiltration. The attack was not opportunistic - investigators found evidence of a sustained intelligence operation targeting the Solana-based trading platform.

The scale and patience of the operation marks it as one of the more sophisticated DeFi attacks on record. North Korean groups have stolen billions from crypto protocols in recent years, using the proceeds to fund state programs. This case reinforces that major DeFi platforms face adversaries with nation-state resources and timelines, not just opportunistic script kiddies.

sources.title: Drift says $270 million exploit was a six-month North Korean intelligence operation
Drift Protocol $280M exploit took 'months of deliberate preparation'

btc.headline: Bitcoin Stuck at $67K as Bearish Sentiment Peaks
btc.body:
Bitcoin is grinding sideways near $67,000 while bearish social media chatter hits a five-week high, according to Santiment data. Sentiment has deteriorated to its worst levels since the Iran war began, yet price has held relatively firm - a dynamic some analysts read as a contrarian signal.

ETF flows are changing how Bitcoin responds to macro conditions. Rather than reacting to Federal Reserve decisions after the fact, institutional buying through spot ETFs appears to be front-running expected policy shifts. Meanwhile, at least one trader argues new price lows are only a matter of time if BTC cannot break out of its current range. Robert Kiyosaki, predictably, used the uncertainty to again recommend Bitcoin and gold.

sources.title: Bitcoin holds steady as sentiment hits worst levels since Iran war began
New Bitcoin price lows 'matter of time' says trader with BTC stuck at $67K
Bitcoin bearish social chatter reaches 5-week high: Santiment
Bitcoin is now front-running the Fed rather than reacting to it. ETFs are the cause
Robert Kiyosaki recommends Bitcoin, gold as 1974 shift comes full circle

btc.headline: AI Is Making Crypto Harder to Secure, Ledger Warns
btc.body:
Ledger's CTO is sounding the alarm on AI-powered threats to crypto security, warning that the same tools improving productivity are also lowering the barrier for sophisticated attacks - from smarter phishing to automated vulnerability discovery. The warning comes as the industry is simultaneously racing to integrate AI more deeply into crypto infrastructure.

Ant Group's blockchain arm is building a platform that lets AI agents transact autonomously on crypto rails, a sign of how quickly the two technologies are converging. The tension is sharp: AI promises to unlock new use cases for blockchain, but it also hands attackers more powerful tools. Anthropic's decision to launch a PAC and engage directly on AI policy in Washington suggests the regulatory stakes around this convergence are rising fast.

sources.title: AI is making crypto's security problem even worse, Ledger CTO warns
Ant Group’s blockchain arm unveils platform for AI agents to transact on crypto rails
Anthropic launches PAC amid tensions with Trump administration over AI policy

btc.headline: Token Supply Glut Threatens Crypto's Long-Term Value
btc.body:
A growing chorus of analysts is flagging an existential problem for the crypto market: token supply is expanding far faster than genuine value creation. New projects continue to launch with aggressive emission schedules, diluting holders and making it harder for any individual asset to sustain price appreciation.

The dynamic helps explain persistent bearish pressure across altcoins even when Bitcoin holds steady. When supply consistently outpaces demand and real utility, token prices face structural headwinds regardless of broader market sentiment. Critics argue the industry has not yet solved the incentive problem - too many projects still rely on inflation to reward participants rather than building products people actually pay to use.

sources.title: ‘Existential’ token problem seen as supply outpaces value creation

food.name: Mafaldine al Limone e Bottarga
food.description: Ruffled ribbon pasta tossed in brown butter, lemon zest, and shaved cured mullet roe for a briny, silky Sunday lunch
food.tip: Grate the bottarga over the pasta off the heat — direct heat turns it bitter and kills the oceanic depth you're paying for

exercise.name: The Broken Clock Run
exercise.description: Run at a genuinely comfortable pace for 20 minutes, then spend the final 25 alternating 90 seconds hard effort with 90 seconds walking — the asymmetric structure stops your brain from gaming the intervals

learn.description: Stochastic resonance is the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding a precise amount of random noise to a weak signal actually makes that signal easier to detect — it appears in neuroscience, climate modelling, and even how crayfish sense predators
learn.why: It quietly dismantles the assumption that noise is always the enemy of clarity, and once you understand it you start noticing its fingerprints in everything from sensory biology to financial markets
